question_text: A scientist investigates how pressure changes with depth in a swimming pool. At the surface (depth 0 m), the pressure is 101,325 Pa. At a depth of 2 m, the measured pressure is 121,725 Pa. Calculate the pressure increase due to the water column alone, then determine the density of water used in this calculation. (Use g = 10 m/s² for this problem.)
correct_answer: Pressure increase = 20,400 Pa, density of water = 1020 kg/m³
explanation: The pressure increase due to water is 121,725 - 101,325 = 20,400 Pa. Using the formula pressure = density × gravity × depth, we have 20,400 = density × 10 × 2, so density = 20,400 ÷ 20 = 1020 kg/m³. This is very close to the accepted value of pure water (1000 kg/m³), with the small difference likely due to dissolved salts or measurement uncertainty.
